title: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group
overall_summary: The paper studies subgroups of the infinite symmetric group S∞ that are effectively closed, comparing this notion with automorphism groups of computable structures, computable Polish presentations, and profinite presentations. Its central results include a priority construction of a Π^0_1 presentation of the two-element group Z2 that is not literally equal to Aut(M) for any computable structure M, contrasted with the theorem that every effectively closed discrete group is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M) for some computable structure M. The paper also proves that certain effectively closed compact subgroups have no computable Polish presentation, while every profinite computable Polish group embeds as an effectively closed subgroup of S∞, and it constructs a Σ^1_1 closed oligomorphic group whose orbit relation is not uniformly hyperarithmetic. For the original problem, the most useful content is the effective-closed viewpoint on automorphism groups of computable structures, the discrete-group/orbit-equivalence analysis via hyperarithmetic bounds, and the coding methods that replace hyperarithmetic or ∅′-computable structures by computable structures while preserving automorphism groups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Introductory observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If C is a computable countable first-order structure with domain ω, in a computable language with computable interpretations of its constants, functions, and relations, then Aut(C), viewed as a subgroup of S∞, is effectively closed. Here S∞ is the group of all permutations of ω, and effectively closed means that the complement is a computably enumerable union of basic open neighborhoods determined by finite partial maps.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper states this as clear. A permutation f is not an automorphism of C exactly when some finite part of f witnesses failure of preservation of the atomic diagram: a constant is sent incorrectly, a relation is not preserved on a finite tuple, or a function value is not preserved on a finite tuple. Because the atomic diagram of C is computable, one can enumerate all finite injective partial maps σ that already violate one of these finite atomic preservation conditions, and enumerate the basic open set of all permutations extending σ into the complement of Aut(C). Conversely, every non-automorphism has such a finite witness. Thus Aut(C) is Π^0_1 as a subset of S∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3 and Claim 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S∞ be the group of all permutations of ω. Let d be the usual ultrametric on ω^ω, and define a complete metric D on S∞ by D(σ,τ) = (d(σ,τ) + d(σ^{-1},τ^{-1}))/2. With the computable dense set of finite-support permutations, this is the natural computable Polish presentation of S∞. A subgroup G ≤ S∞ is effectively closed in the sense that G = P ∩ S∞ for some Π^0_1 subset P of ω^ω if and only if G is an effectively closed subset of the computable Polish space (S∞,D). Equivalently, the metric D on S∞ is effectively compatible with the standard Baire-space metric d on ω^ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof gives uniform translations between basic open sets in ω^ω and basic D-open sets in S∞. If U ⊆ ω^ω is a basic open set specified by a finite partial map σ, then if σ is not injective, U ∩ S∞ is empty. If σ is injective, enumerate all finite injective extensions τ_i of σ^{-1} whose domains are finite initial segments of ω. The pairs (τ_i^{-1},τ_i) specify basic D-open neighborhoods in S∞, and their union is exactly U ∩ S∞. Conversely, if V is a basic D-open set described by finite information about a permutation and its inverse, in the simplified form of a finite permutation σ of an initial segment together with σ^{-1}, then the corresponding basic open U in ω^ω is the set of all extensions of σ, and U ∩ S∞ = V. These translations are effective, so an enumeration of the complement of P in ω^ω can be transformed into an enumeration of the complement of P ∩ S∞ in (S∞,D), and conversely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Fact 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every Σ^0_2 set S ⊆ ω, there is a uniform procedure which, given x ∈ ω, outputs a computable linear order isomorphic to ω if x ∈ S and outputs a computable linear order isomorphic to ω^2 if x ∉ S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as folklore from computable structure theory and does not prove it. It is used as a uniform coding device: membership in a Σ^0_2 predicate is represented by choosing between two rigid computable linear order types, ω and ω^2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every ∅′-computable relational structure A, with computable underlying set and computable language, there is a computable structure B such that Aut(A) and Aut(B) are topologically isomorphic.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction is a Marker-style blow-up using rigid linear orders. For each n-ary relation symbol P of A and each tuple ā ∈ A^n, add a new infinite set C^P_ā of elements, with these sets pairwise disjoint. Add relations to B that say y ∈ C^P_x̄, thereby linking the blow-up fiber to the tuple x̄. For each tuple ā, put a linear order L^P_ā on C^P_ā. Using Fact 2.4 and the fact that P(ā) is ∅′-computable, hence Δ^0_2 and in particular expressible for this purpose by a Σ^0_2 condition, choose L^P_ā to be a computable copy of ω if A ⊨ P(ā), and a computable copy of ω^2 otherwise. Add to B the relation saying y1,y2 ∈ C^P_x̄ and y1 <_{L^P_x̄} y2. The resulting B has a computable copy because the linear orders are produced uniformly. Both ω and ω^2 are rigid, so an automorphism of B is completely determined by its restriction to the copy of A, and every automorphism of A extends uniquely fiber-by-fiber to an automorphism of B, since it sends tuples satisfying P to tuples satisfying P and non-satisfying tuples to non-satisfying tuples. The restriction map Φ: Aut(B) → Aut(A) is therefore a group isomorphism. It is topological: continuity is immediate from restriction, and openness is checked on subbasic clopen sets of Aut(B). If a finite condition maps an element q ∈ C^P_ā to p ∈ C^P_b̄, then its image under Φ imposes the finite condition ā ↦ b̄ on Aut(A); because the fiber orders are rigid, this exactly captures the effect of mapping q to p whenever the condition is consistent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.2(1)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists an effectively closed subgroup P ≤ S∞ which is topologically isomorphic to the two-element cyclic group Z2, but such that P is not equal, as a concrete subgroup of S∞, to Aut(C) for any computable structure C on domain ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof builds a Π^0_1 subgroup P by enumerating basic neighborhoods into its complement while diagonalizing against a computable list (M_e)_{e∈ω} of all partial computable structures on domain ω. A finite injective partial map is identified with the basic neighborhood of permutations extending it. A finite map x̄ → ȳ is called nice if it is a finite permutation of an initial segment of ω and has order 2; the notation x̄ ↔ ȳ emphasizes this. The construction starts with a nice neighborhood of the identity, for example (0,1) ↔ (0,1), and a disjoint nice neighborhood intended to contain the nonidentity element, for example (0,1) ↔ (1,0). It continuously forbids all subneighborhoods of the identity side that do not contain Id_ω. Each diagonalization strategy for M_e works inside a currently assigned nice neighborhood σ_e = x̄ ↔ ȳ on the nonidentity side. Let n = length(x̄). The strategy first forbids the basic neighborhood x̄ n → x̄(n+1). It then waits for M_e to separate some equal-length extensions x̄ā of x̄ n and x̄b̄ of x̄(n+1) by a first-order atomic formula φ. While waiting, it lets lower-priority strategies work inside the nice neighborhood x̄ n ↔ ȳ n, and it gradually forbids other subneighborhoods of σ_e of the form x̄ n → ȳ k with k ≠ n. If M_e is total but no such atomic separation ever appears, then a back-and-forth argument extends x̄ n → x̄(n+1) to an automorphism of M_e; since this neighborhood has already been forbidden from P, this ensures P ≠ Aut(M_e). If a separating atomic formula φ is found, the strategy chooses a tuple c̄ of very large fresh numbers, of the same length as ā and b̄, so that the partial maps x̄ā → ȳc̄ and ȳc̄ → x̄b̄ have not yet been forbidden and can be extended to nice finite involutions. Let N1 and N2 be the corresponding nice subneighborhoods. The strategy stops the previous slow forbidding, forbids the rest of x̄ n ↔ ȳ n, begins forbidding extensions of N2 one by one, and allows lower-priority work inside N1. It then waits for M_e to evaluate φ on ȳc̄. If φ witnesses x̄ā not mapping to ȳc̄ in Aut(M_e), then Aut(M_e) ∩ N1 = ∅, so the construction keeps the eventual nonidentity element inside N1 while eventually forbidding N2. If instead φ witnesses ȳc̄ not mapping to x̄b̄, then Aut(M_e) ∩ N2 = ∅; the construction stops forbidding N2, forbids N1, chooses a nice subneighborhood τ ⊆ N2, and moves all lower-priority work into τ. If M_e never evaluates φ, then M_e is not a total computable structure, so it need not be diagonalized against. Priority is handled by ordering strategies by e and initializing all weaker strategies whenever a stronger strategy changes the neighborhood in which weaker strategies may act. On initialization, a weaker strategy chooses a fresh nice subneighborhood inside the currently permitted region, with diameter at most 2^{-i} for the i-th strategy. The verification uses three key points: first, if M_e is total and x̄ n → x̄(n+1) cannot extend to an automorphism, then some finite extensions are separated by an atomic formula; second, the tuple c̄ can be chosen large enough that the relevant nice involutive neighborhoods have not been forbidden; third, each strategy eventually stabilizes in one nice neighborhood. The nested stable neighborhoods have diameters tending to 0, so their intersection contains a single permutation Θ. Each finite approximation is an involution, hence Θ^2 = Id_ω. The final Π^0_1 set is P = {Id_ω, Θ}, so P is a subgroup isomorphic to Z2, and every total computable M_e is diagonalized against.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Claims 3.2, 3.3, and 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Within the proof of Theorem 0.2(1), the following claims are established. Claim 3.2: If M_e is total and a finite partial map x̄ n → x̄(n+1) does not extend to an automorphism of M_e, then there are finite equal-length extensions x̄ā and x̄b̄ of x̄ n and x̄(n+1), respectively, and a first-order atomic formula φ separating x̄ā from x̄b̄ in M_e. Claim 3.3: If the strategy reaches the stage where such φ, ā, and b̄ have been found, then one can choose a tuple c̄ of fresh sufficiently large numbers so that the partial maps x̄ā → ȳc̄ and ȳc̄ → x̄b̄ extend to nice finite involutions N1 and N2 that have not yet been forbidden. Claim 3.5: If the e-th strategy is never initialized after a stage s, then after some later stage s′ it has a stable nice neighborhood N_e such that all weaker-priority strategies act only inside N_e.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Claim 3.2 is a finite back-and-forth argument: if no finite atomic separation existed, then every finite partial isomorphism extending x̄ n → x̄(n+1) could be extended back and forth, yielding an automorphism of the total structure M_e extending the forbidden map, contrary to the hypothesis. Claim 3.3 uses that at any finite stage only finitely many basic neighborhoods have been forbidden. By taking c̄ with entries sufficiently large and fresh, the construction avoids all already forbidden finite restrictions. The partial maps can then be completed to finite involutions by adding reciprocal pairs whenever one direction has already been specified and fixing all remaining points of the finite initial segment; this produces nice neighborhoods inside the ambient x̄ ↔ ȳ. Claim 3.5 follows from the finite-outcome nature of the strategy: it may wait forever at the first waiting stage, in which case the stable neighborhood is x̄ n ↔ ȳ n; or it may find φ and either stay forever in N1 or make one final switch to a subneighborhood of N2. Thus a strategy that is no longer initialized eventually stops changing the region assigned to lower-priority strategies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Facts 2.1 and 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fact 2.1, cited from Melnikov: every computable Polish presentation of a profinite group P can be transformed into a 0′-computable inverse system F0 ← F1 ← F2 ← ⋯ of finite groups with surjective bonding maps whose inverse limit is topologically isomorphic to P. Fact 2.2, cited from La Roche: every recursively presented profinite group is isomorphic to Gal(K/N), where K is a computably presented algebraic extension of Q and N is a computable subfield of K. The paper uses a fully relativized form of Fact 2.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Both facts are used as black boxes, and their proofs are not included in the paper. Fact 2.1 converts computable Polish information about a profinite group into an effective inverse-limit presentation, with one jump. Fact 2.2 realizes an effective inverse system of finite groups as a Galois group of a computable algebraic field extension over a computable subfield; the relativized version allows 0′-computable fields and subfields.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Part (1): There exists an effectively closed compact, equivalently profinite, subgroup of S∞ that has no computable Polish presentation and hence no recursive profinite presentation. Part (2): Every profinite group that has a computable Polish presentation is topologically isomorphic to an effectively closed subgroup of S∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For part (1), for a set S of prime numbers define P_S = ∏_{p∈S} Z_p, the product of cyclic groups of order p. If P_S had a computable Polish presentation, Fact 2.1 would give a 0′-computable inverse system for P_S. Using a result cited from Melnikov, the paper then uniformly obtains a 0′-computable presentation of the discrete Pontryagin dual ⊕_{p∈S} Z_p. From this dual one can 0′-computably list the prime orders of elements, showing that S is Σ^0_2. Therefore choose a Π^0_2-complete set S of primes, so S is not Σ^0_2. The paper builds a computable graph M with Aut(M) topologically isomorphic to P_S. For each prime p, include a connected component C_p consisting of a p-cycle x^p_0 - x^p_1 - ⋯ - x^p_{p-1} - x^p_0, and attach to each cycle node x^p_i a finite or infinite chain. The lengths are controlled by a computable approximation to the Π^0_2 predicate for p: if p ∉ S and the predicate fires only finitely many times, say s, the i-th chain has finite length s+i, making C_p rigid; if p ∈ S, each of the p chains is infinite, so rotations of the p-cycle give Aut(C_p) ≅ Z_p. Since components do not interact, Aut(M) ≅ ∏_{p∈S} Aut(C_p) ≅ P_S, and the product topology agrees with the permutation topology because finite values determine subbasic clopen conditions. Since M is computable, Aut(M) is effectively closed, but P_S cannot have a computable Polish presentation by the previous paragraph. For part (2), start with a profinite computable Polish group P. Fact 2.1 gives a 0′-recursive inverse system. By the relativized La Roche theorem, obtain a 0′-computable algebraic field K and 0′-computable subfield N with Aut(K/N) ≅ P. By passing to a 0′-computably isomorphic copy, assume N is computable. Name each element of N by a singleton unary predicate and replace field operations by their relational graphs, obtaining a 0′-computable relational structure F with Aut(F) ≅ P. Apply Proposition 2.3 to obtain a computable structure F̂ with Aut(F̂) ≅ Aut(F). Hence P is topologically isomorphic to Aut(F̂), an effectively closed subgroup of S∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G ≤ S∞ be a discrete effectively closed subgroup. Define the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples by ā ∼_G b̄ if ā and b̄ have the same length and there exists σ ∈ G such that σ(ā) = b̄. Then ∼_G is hyperarithmetic, uniformly in the tuple length.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 1.3, G is a Π^0_2 subset of ω^ω. Since G is discrete as a subspace of the second-countable Polish group S∞, it is countable and hence has no perfect subset. The proof uses the effective perfect set theorem for Σ^1_1 subsets of ω^ω: if a Σ^1_1 set A has no perfect subset, then all elements of A are hyperarithmetic; by Spector Σ^1_1-bounding, there is a computable ordinal γ such that every element of A is computable from 0^(γ). Applying this to G, all elements of G are 0^(γ)-computable. Because G is Π^0_2 as a subset of ω^ω and all its members are bounded in this way, 0^(γ+2) computes a listing of all elements of G. Then 0^(γ+3) decides whether a given pair of finite tuples lies in the same G-orbit by searching this listing for a permutation sending one tuple to the other and using one more jump to decide the resulting existential question. Thus ∼_G is hyperarithmetic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every closed subgroup G ≤ S∞, there is a countable structure M such that G = Aut(M), and M is computable from the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard construction realizing a closed subgroup as an automorphism group. For each n and for every ∼_G-equivalence class of n-tuples, introduce an n-ary relation symbol whose interpretation is exactly that orbit. The resulting structure M has domain ω and is computable relative to ∼_G because membership in each relation is determined by orbit equivalence. Every element of G preserves all these orbit relations, so G ⊆ Aut(M). Conversely, if π ∈ Aut(M), then for every finite tuple ā, the tuple π(ā) lies in the same G-orbit as ā, so there is some g_ā ∈ G agreeing with π on ā. Thus every finite restriction of π is realized by an element of G. Since G is closed in S∞, π belongs to G. Therefore Aut(M) = G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every hyperarithmetic structure M, there is a computable structure N such that Aut(N) is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof generalizes Proposition 2.3. Suppose M is Δ^0_γ for some computable ordinal γ. The paper invokes a generalization of the linear-order coding fact: membership in a Σ^0_α class can be uniformly coded into the isomorphism type of one of two rigid linear orders that agree on all computable Π_γ infinitary formulas but are separated at a higher level; for the purposes of the argument, one may use a copy of ω^{γ+1} if a relevant Σ^0_γ fact holds and a copy of ω^{γ+2} otherwise. Then repeat the blow-up construction from Proposition 2.3: for each atomic relation and each tuple, attach a rigid linear-order fiber whose type records whether that relation holds of that tuple. The fibers are rigid, so automorphisms of the expanded computable structure are determined exactly by their restrictions to the original domain, and every automorphism of M extends uniquely to the expansion. The same clopen-neighborhood argument as in Proposition 2.3 gives a topological isomorphism of automorphism groups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G ≤ S∞ is an effectively closed subgroup and the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples is hyperarithmetic, then there is a computable structure M such that G is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Lemma 4.3 to the closed subgroup G. This gives a structure A with Aut(A) = G and with atomic diagram computable from ∼_G. If ∼_G is hyperarithmetic, then A is a hyperarithmetic structure. Lemma 4.4 then produces a computable structure M with Aut(M) topologically isomorphic to Aut(A). Hence Aut(M) is topologically isomorphic to G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.2(2)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every effectively closed discrete group, meaning every discrete topological group presented as an effectively closed subgroup of S∞, is topologically isomorphic to Aut(M) for some computable structure M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let G ≤ S∞ be effectively closed and discrete. By Proposition 4.1, its orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples is hyperarithmetic. By Proposition 4.2, any effectively closed subgroup of S∞ with hyperarithmetic orbit equivalence relation is topologically isomorphic to the automorphism group of a computable structure. Combining these two propositions yields the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 4.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G is an effectively closed discrete group, then G has a hyperarithmetical presentation as a countable discrete group in the sense of computable structure theory. Conversely, the paper outlines that a countable discrete group with a hyperarithmetical presentation can be represented, up to topological isomorphism, as the automorphism group of a computable structure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the converse direction sketched in the paper, let G be a countable discrete group with hyperarithmetical diagram. Use Cayley's theorem: map each g ∈ G to the permutation σ_g of the underlying set of G given by σ_g(h) = gh. The image H ≤ S∞ is topologically isomorphic to G and is discrete, because σ_g is isolated by the basic condition sending the identity element e to g. Moreover H is arithmetical in the diagram of G, so its orbit equivalence relation ∼_H is hyperarithmetic. Lemma 4.3 gives a hyperarithmetic structure M with H ≅ Aut(M), and Lemma 4.4 gives a computable structure N with Aut(N) ≅ Aut(M). Thus G ≅ H ≅ Aut(N).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 0.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists a Σ^1_1 closed oligomorphic subgroup G ≤ S∞ such that its orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on finite tuples is not hyperarithmetic. Oligomorphic means that for each n there are only finitely many G-orbits on n-tuples.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction defines an orbit equivalence relation first, then takes the closed group preserving it. For a nondecreasing sequence k = ⟨k_n⟩ of natural numbers, define an equivalence relation ∼_k on finite tuples. For injective tuples ā,b̄ of the same length n, set ā ∼_k b̄ if for every m < n there are at least n−m coordinates i < n such that a_i ≡ b_i mod 2^{k_m}. For non-injective tuples, the paper says to extend this in the obvious equality-pattern-respecting way. The paper verifies that ∼_k is an equivalence relation, is invariant under coordinate permutations, is preserved under taking corresponding subsequences, has the back-and-forth property, and has only finitely many classes of n-tuples for each n. Let G_k be the set of all f ∈ S∞ such that whenever f(ā)=b̄, one has ā ∼_k b̄. Then G_k is a closed subgroup of S∞; the listed properties imply that ∼_k is exactly the orbit equivalence relation of G_k and that G_k is oligomorphic. To make ∼_k non-hyperarithmetic but Σ^1_1, the proof works over the admissible structure L_{ω_1^ck}. Since the Σ_1-projectum of L_{ω_1^ck} is ω, choose a Δ_2(L_{ω_1^ck}) increasing cofinal sequence ⟨α_n⟩_{n<ω} in ω_1^ck with a finite-change approximation ⟨α_{n,s}⟩_{n<ω,s<ω_1^ck}: it is Δ_1(L_{ω_1^ck}), continuous at limit stages, and for each n changes only finitely many times. For s ≤ ω_1^ck define k_{n,s} = ∑_{m≤n} #{t < s : α_{m,t} ≠ α_{m,t+1}}. Let k = k_{ω_1^ck}. The use of moduli 2^{k_m} gives the needed monotone nesting of the relations as s increases, so the final relation ∼_k is Π_1 over L_{ω_1^ck}, which the paper identifies here as Σ^1_1. Hence G_k is Σ^1_1. Finally, ∼_k is not hyperarithmetic: from ∼_k, over the appropriate admissible set, one can recover the cofinal sequence ⟨α_n⟩. Specifically, for each n and s < ω_1^ck, if the number of ∼_{k_s}-equivalence classes of n-tuples is the same as the final number of ∼_k-classes, then α_n = α_{n,s}. Thus ω_1^{∼_k} > ω_1^ck, so ∼_k is not hyperarithmetic.

proof_techniques.name: Automorphism groups as Π^0_1 subgroups of S∞
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the basic fact that a computable structure has an effectively closed automorphism group: non-automorphisms are detected by finite failures of atomic preservation, and these finite failures enumerate basic open neighborhoods in the complement. This allows computable-structure questions about automorphisms to be recast as questions about Π^0_1 closed subgroups of the infinite symmetric group.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a computable representation A in the original problem, Aut(A) can be studied as a Π^0_1 subgroup of S∞. If computable AUT-countability forces this closed group to be countable/discrete, the paper's discrete effectively closed group analysis becomes directly relevant.

proof_techniques.name: Effective compatibility of Baire-space and permutation-space presentations
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 1.3 shows how to move effectively between Π^0_1 subsets of ω^ω and Π^0_1 subsets of the natural computable Polish presentation of S∞. The proof explicitly translates finite partial maps in ω^ω into finite information about a permutation and its inverse, and conversely.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful whenever one wants to define or diagonalize against automorphisms by finite partial maps while still claiming that the resulting automorphism set is effectively closed in the correct topology.

proof_techniques.name: Priority diagonalization inside nested involutive neighborhoods
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 0.2(1) constructs a Π^0_1 subgroup isomorphic to Z2 by maintaining a nested sequence of finite involutive neighborhoods for the intended nonidentity element while enumerating all incompatible neighborhoods into the complement. Each requirement diagonalizes against one computable structure by forcing either a forbidden finite partial automorphism to exist in that structure or forcing the eventual nonidentity permutation to lie in a neighborhood disjoint from that structure's automorphism group. Initialization and shrinking neighborhoods ensure finite injury and convergence to a single involution.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar finite-injury construction could be adapted to build computable structures or automorphism groups satisfying requirements against all candidate Σ^in_1 definitions of automorphism graphs, while preserving that the final nontrivial automorphisms remain computable or otherwise controlled.

proof_techniques.name: Intermediate fresh tuple splitting trick
proof_techniques.description: A key move in the Z2 priority construction is: once a finite map x̄ā → x̄b̄ is known not to be an automorphism of a target structure, choose a very large fresh tuple c̄ and factor the obstruction through two possible maps x̄ā → ȳc̄ and ȳc̄ → x̄b̄. When the target structure eventually evaluates the atomic formula on ȳc̄, at least one of these two maps is impossible; the construction then keeps the final permutation inside the impossible side and forbids the other side as needed. The largeness of c̄ ensures that both candidate neighborhoods are still available and can be extended to finite involutions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This trick is a portable way to delay commitment until an opponent reveals finite information. It may help in diagonalizing against existential infinitary definitions: introduce a fresh tuple so that any eventual witness or evaluation determines which branch can be used to avoid definability.

proof_techniques.name: Rigid-order blow-up preserving automorphism groups
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.3 and Lemma 4.4 use a Marker-style expansion: attach to each tuple a rigid fiber whose isomorphism type encodes whether a relation holds of that tuple. Because the fibers are rigid, automorphisms are exactly the original automorphisms extended uniquely to the fibers. By choosing the fiber types using effective coding of Σ^0_2 or hyperarithmetic information, one converts a noncomputable presentation into a computable structure without changing the automorphism group up to topological isomorphism.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a powerful way to first design a structure with the desired automorphism group using higher-level information, then replace it by a computable structure while preserving automorphisms. It may be especially useful for building examples on a cone or for coding parameter-dependent information without adding unwanted automorphisms.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical orbit-relation structure for a closed subgroup
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.3 realizes any closed subgroup G ≤ S∞ as Aut(M) by adding one relation for each orbit of G on finite tuples. The closure of G is crucial: any permutation preserving all orbit relations has each finite restriction realized by some element of G, and hence belongs to G. The resulting structure is computable from the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can first define a suitable closed group of permutations with controlled computability properties, this lemma gives a canonical structure realizing it. The complexity of ∼_G then determines whether Lemma 4.4 can turn that structure into a computable one.

proof_techniques.name: Effective perfect set and Spector boundedness for discrete Π^0_1 groups
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 4.1 applies the effective perfect set theorem and Spector Σ^1_1-bounding to show that a discrete effectively closed subgroup of S∞ has all its elements hyperarithmetically bounded, and therefore has hyperarithmetic orbit equivalence. The argument uses discreteness to rule out perfect subsets and effective closedness to place the group low enough in the projective hierarchy.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Computably AUT-countable structures have only countably many automorphisms; for closed automorphism groups this often points toward discreteness. This technique may provide uniform hyperarithmetic bounds on automorphisms or orbits, which could interact with attempts to prove or refute Σ^in_1 definability.

proof_techniques.name: Graph components realizing products of finite cyclic groups
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 0.3(1), the paper realizes ∏_{p∈S} Z_p as the automorphism group of a computable graph by using one component for each prime p. A p-cycle with identical infinite chains attached to each vertex has rotational automorphism group Z_p, while carefully chosen unequal finite chain lengths make the component rigid. A Π^0_2 approximation controls whether the chains become infinite or stabilize at unequal finite lengths.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a concrete template for computable structures whose automorphism groups are prescribed products or sums of simple finite symmetries. Variants could be used to build examples with many individually controlled automorphisms while retaining computability of the presentation.

proof_techniques.name: Oligomorphic orbit relations from congruence patterns and finite-change approximations
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 0.4 constructs an oligomorphic orbit equivalence relation by defining finite-tuple equivalence via congruence modulo powers of two, with finitely many classes at each arity. A finite-change approximation to a cofinal sequence in ω_1^ck is used to make the full orbit relation non-hyperarithmetic while keeping the acting group closed and Σ^1_1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one investigates highly homogeneous or ω-categorical candidates for the original problem, this construction indicates how orbit relations can be individually finite at each arity yet nonuniformly complex. It also signals a potential obstacle: oligomorphic behavior alone does not guarantee uniform hyperarithmetic control outside the Π^0_1 setting.

other_useful_info: The paper cites the classical result, referenced to Gao [Gao09], that every closed subgroup of S∞ is the automorphism group of some countable structure; Lemma 4.3 gives the effective orbit-relation version of this construction. The paper leaves open broader extension questions, including whether the positive realization theorem for effectively closed discrete groups extends beyond the cases handled, whether Theorem 0.3(2) extends from profinite computable Polish groups to arbitrary closed subgroups of S∞, and whether a Π^0_1 oligomorphic group can witness the kind of non-realizability phenomenon in Theorem 0.2(1). The paper also suggests, but does not develop, extending the notion of effectively closed subgroup from S∞ to more general computable Polish group presentations.
why_relevant: Automorphism groups of countable structures are closed subgroups of the infinite symmetric group, and computable presentations make them effectively closed. Results about computable elements, presentations, and complexity of such subgroups are directly applicable to computably AUT-countable structures.
